question_text: Find the area of the region bounded by the ellipse {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{4} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{9} = 1{/tex} .
explanation: {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{4} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{9} = 1{/tex} {tex} = 4\int_0^2 {\frac{2}{3}\sqrt {9 - {y^2}} dx} {/tex} {tex} = 4\int_0^2 {\frac{2}{3}\sqrt {{3^2} - {y^2}} dx} {/tex} {tex}=4×\frac{2}{3} \left[ {\frac{y}{2}\sqrt{9-y^2}+\frac{9}{2}sin^{-1}\frac{y}{3}} \right]_0^3{/tex} {tex}=4×\frac{2}{3} \left[ {\frac{3}{2}(0)+\frac{9}{2}sin^{-1}(1)- }\left[ {0-0} \right] \right]{/tex} {tex}=4×\frac{2}{3} \left[ {\frac{9}{2}(\frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{2})} \right]{/tex} {tex} = 6\pi {/tex} sq. units
solution: {tex}\frac{{{x^2}}}{4} + \frac{{{y^2}}}{9} = 1{/tex} {tex} = 4\int_0^2 {\frac{2}{3}\sqrt {9 - {y^2}} dx} {/tex} {tex} = 4\int_0^2 {\frac{2}{3}\sqrt {{3^2} - {y^2}} dx} {/tex} {tex}=4×\frac{2}{3} \left[ {\frac{y}{2}\sqrt{9-y^2}+\frac{9}{2}sin^{-1}\frac{y}{3}} \right]_0^3{/tex} {tex}=4×\frac{2}{3} \left[ {\frac{3}{2}(0)+\frac{9}{2}sin^{-1}(1)- }\left[ {0-0} \right] \right]{/tex} {tex}=4×\frac{2}{3} \left[ {\frac{9}{2}(\frac{ {tex}\pi{/tex} }{2})} \right]{/tex} {tex} = 6\pi {/tex} sq. units
solution_html:

{tex} = 4\int_0^2 {\frac{2}{3}\sqrt {9 - {y^2}} dx} {/tex} 
{tex} = 4\int_0^2 {\frac{2}{3}\sqrt {{3^2} - {y^2}} dx} {/tex} 
{tex}=4×\frac{2}{3} \left[ {\frac{y}{2}\sqrt{9-y^2}+\frac{9}{2}sin^{-1}\frac{y}{3}} \right]_0^3{/tex} 
{tex}=4×\frac{2}{3} \left[ {\frac{3}{2}(0)+\frac{9}{2}sin^{-1}(1)- }\left[ {0-0} \right] \right]{/tex}

{tex} = 6\pi {/tex} sq. units

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Consider the curve x2 + y2 = 16 and line y = x in the first quadrant.

  1. Find the point of intersection of both the given curves. (1)
  2. Evaluate {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} xdx. (1)
  3. Find the value of the integral {tex}\int_\limits{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4} \sqrt{16-x^{2}} d x{/tex}. (2)
    OR
    Find the area bounded by the two given curves. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Consider the curve x 2 + y 2 = 16 and line y = x in the first quadrant. Find the point of intersection of both the given curves. (1) Evaluate {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}}{/tex} xdx. (1) Find the value of the integral {tex}\int_\limits{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4} \sqrt{16-x^{2}} d x{/tex} . (2) OR Find the area bounded by the two given curves. (2)
explanation: We have, x 2 + y 2 = 16 ...(i) and y = x ...(ii) From (i) and (ii), 2x 2 = 16 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}2\sqrt{2}{/tex} ( {tex}\therefore{/tex} x lies in first quadrant) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Point of intersection of (i) and (ii) in first quadrant is {tex}(2\sqrt{2}, 2\sqrt{2}){/tex} {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}} x d x=\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}}{2}=\frac{8}{2}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\int_\limits{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4} \sqrt{16-x^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{16-x^{2}}+\frac{16}{2} \cdot \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{4}\right)\right]_{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4}{/tex} = 8 sin -1 (1) - 4 - 8 sin -1 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}8\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)-4-8\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} - 4 - 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} - 4 = 2( {tex}\pi{/tex} - 2) OR Required area = Area (OLA) + Area (BAL) = {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}} x d x+\int_\limits{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4} \sqrt{16-x^{2}} d x{/tex} = 4 + 2( {tex}\pi{/tex} - 2) = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} sq. units
solution: We have, x 2 + y 2 = 16 ...(i) and y = x ...(ii) From (i) and (ii), 2x 2 = 16 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}2\sqrt{2}{/tex} ( {tex}\therefore{/tex} x lies in first quadrant) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Point of intersection of (i) and (ii) in first quadrant is {tex}(2\sqrt{2}, 2\sqrt{2}){/tex} {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}} x d x=\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}}{2}=\frac{8}{2}{/tex} = 4 {tex}\int_\limits{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4} \sqrt{16-x^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{16-x^{2}}+\frac{16}{2} \cdot \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{4}\right)\right]_{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4}{/tex} = 8 sin -1 (1) - 4 - 8 sin -1 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex} = {tex}8\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)-4-8\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex} = 4 {tex}\pi{/tex} - 4 - 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} - 4 = 2( {tex}\pi{/tex} - 2) OR Required area = Area (OLA) + Area (BAL) = {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}} x d x+\int_\limits{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4} \sqrt{16-x^{2}} d x{/tex} = 4 + 2( {tex}\pi{/tex} - 2) = 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} sq. units
solution_html:
1. We have, x2 + y2 = 16 ...(i)
    and y = x ...(ii)
    From (i) and (ii), 2x2 = 16 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}2\sqrt{2}{/tex} ({tex}\therefore{/tex} x lies in first quadrant)
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Point of intersection of (i) and (ii) in first quadrant is {tex}(2\sqrt{2}, 2\sqrt{2}){/tex}

  2. {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}} x d x=\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}}=\frac{(2 \sqrt{2})^{2}}{2}=\frac{8}{2}{/tex} = 4

  3. {tex}\int_\limits{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4} \sqrt{16-x^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{16-x^{2}}+\frac{16}{2} \cdot \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{4}\right)\right]_{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4}{/tex}
    = 8 sin-1(1) - 4 - 8 sin-1{tex}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}8\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)-4-8\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right){/tex}
    = 4{tex}\pi{/tex} - 4 - 2{tex}\pi{/tex} = 2{tex}\pi{/tex} - 4 = 2({tex}\pi{/tex} - 2)
    OR

    Required area = Area (OLA) + Area (BAL)
    = {tex}\int_\limits{0}^{2 \sqrt{2}} x d x+\int_\limits{2 \sqrt{2}}^{4} \sqrt{16-x^{2}} d x{/tex}
    = 4 + 2({tex}\pi{/tex} - 2) = 2{tex}\pi{/tex} sq. units

(i), we get Area of region ABCD = 4 + 4 = 8 sq. units OR {tex}\int_\limits 0^{1 / 2} \sqrt{1-(x-1)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\frac{x-1}{2} \sqrt{1-(x-1)^2}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x-1}{1}\right)\right]_0^{1 / 2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}-1\right) \sqrt{1-\frac{1}{4}}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)-\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex} (0) - {tex}\frac 12{/tex} sin -1 (-1) = {tex}\left[\frac{-1}{4} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{6}+0+\frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{8}-\frac{\pi}{12}+\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{8}{/tex}

1. Equation of line AB is
    y - 0 = {tex}\frac{3-0}{1-(-1)}{/tex}[x - (-1)]

    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y - 3 = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}(x - 1)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\frac {-1} 2{/tex} x + {tex}\frac 72{/tex}

  3. Area of region ABCD

    Now, area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ABC
    = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}| -1(3 - 2) + 1(2 - 0) +3(0 - 3)|
    = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}|-1 + 2 - 9|
    = {tex}\frac 12{/tex}{tex}\times{/tex}8 = 4 sq. units
    and Area of {tex}\triangle{/tex}ADC = {tex}\frac 12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} base {tex}\times{/tex} height
    = {tex}\frac12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} AD {tex}\times{/tex} CD = {tex}\frac12{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 2

    = {tex}\left[\frac{x-1}{2} \sqrt{1-(x-1)^2}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x-1}{1}\right)\right]_0^{1 / 2}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{1}{2}-1\right) \sqrt{1-\frac{1}{4}}+\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)-\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right){/tex}(0) -{tex}\frac 12{/tex}sin-1 (-1)
    = {tex}\left[\frac{-1}{4} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\pi}{6}+0+\frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{8}-\frac{\pi}{12}+\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\pi}{6}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{8}{/tex}

A child cut a pizza with a knife. Pizza is circular in shape which is represented by x2 + y2 = 4 and sharp edge of knife represents a straight line given by x = {tex}\sqrt{3}y{/tex}.
 

  1. Find the point(s) of the intersection of the edge of a knife (line) and pizza shown in the figure. (1)
  2. Draw the smaller area bounded by pizza and edge of the knife in the first quadrant. (1)
  3. Find the value of area of the region bounded by circular pizza and edge of knife in first quadrant. (2)
    OR
    Area of each slice of pizza when child cut the pizza into 4 equal pieces. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: A child cut a pizza with a knife. Pizza is circular in shape which is represented by x 2 + y 2 = 4 and sharp edge of knife represents a straight line given by x = {tex}\sqrt{3}y{/tex} . Find the point(s) of the intersection of the edge of a knife (line) and pizza shown in the figure. (1) Draw the smaller area bounded by pizza and edge of the knife in the first quadrant. (1) Find the value of area of the region bounded by circular pizza and edge of knife in first quadrant. (2) OR Area of each slice of pizza when child cut the pizza into 4 equal pieces. (2)
explanation: We have, x 2 + y 2 = 4 ...(i) and x = {tex}\sqrt{3}y{/tex} ...(ii) From (i) and (ii), we get 3y 2 + y 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4y 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\pm1{/tex} From (ii), x = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} , {tex}-\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Points of intersection of pizza and edge of knife are ( {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} , 1), ( {tex}-\sqrt{3}{/tex} , -1). Required area = {tex}\int_{0}^{\sqrt{3}} \frac{x}{\sqrt{3}} d x+\int_{\sqrt{3}}^{2} \sqrt{4-x^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{\sqrt{3}}+\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{4-x^{2}}+\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]_{\sqrt{3}}^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\left[\frac{3}{2}-0\right]+\left[2 \sin ^{-1}(1)-\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+2 \sin ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{2 \pi}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} sq. units OR We have, x 2 + y 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 0) 2 + (y - 0) 2 = (2) 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius = 2 Area of {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} th slice of pizza = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} (2) 2 = {tex}\pi{/tex} sq. units
solution: We have, x 2 + y 2 = 4 ...(i) and x = {tex}\sqrt{3}y{/tex} ...(ii) From (i) and (ii), we get 3y 2 + y 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4y 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\pm1{/tex} From (ii), x = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} , {tex}-\sqrt{3}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Points of intersection of pizza and edge of knife are ( {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex} , 1), ( {tex}-\sqrt{3}{/tex} , -1). Required area = {tex}\int_{0}^{\sqrt{3}} \frac{x}{\sqrt{3}} d x+\int_{\sqrt{3}}^{2} \sqrt{4-x^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{\sqrt{3}}+\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{4-x^{2}}+\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]_{\sqrt{3}}^{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\left[\frac{3}{2}-0\right]+\left[2 \sin ^{-1}(1)-\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+2 \sin ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{2 \pi}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex} sq. units OR We have, x 2 + y 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 0) 2 + (y - 0) 2 = (2) 2 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius = 2 Area of {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} th slice of pizza = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} {tex}\pi{/tex} (2) 2 = {tex}\pi{/tex} sq. units
solution_html:
1. We have, x2 + y2 = 4 ...(i)
    and x = {tex}\sqrt{3}y{/tex} ...(ii)
    From (i) and (ii), we get
    3y2 + y2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4y2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\pm1{/tex}
    From (ii), x = {tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}, {tex}-\sqrt{3}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Points of intersection of pizza and edge of knife are ({tex}\sqrt{3}{/tex}, 1), ({tex}-\sqrt{3}{/tex}, -1).
  3. Required area = {tex}\int_{0}^{\sqrt{3}} \frac{x}{\sqrt{3}} d x+\int_{\sqrt{3}}^{2} \sqrt{4-x^{2}} d x{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{\sqrt{3}}+\left[\frac{x}{2} \sqrt{4-x^{2}}+\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2}\right)\right]_{\sqrt{3}}^{2}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\left[\frac{3}{2}-0\right]+\left[2 \sin ^{-1}(1)-\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+2 \sin ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\right]{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}+\frac{2 \pi}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}-\frac{2 \pi}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}sq. units
    OR
    We have, x2 + y2 = 4
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x - 0)2 + (y - 0)2 = (2)2
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Radius = 2
    Area of {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}th slice of pizza = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}{tex}\pi{/tex}(2)2 = {tex}\pi{/tex} sq. units

question_html: Using method of integration find the area of the triangle ABC, co-ordinates of whose vertices are A (2, 0), B (4, 5) and C (6, 3).
question_text: Using method of integration find the area of the triangle ABC, co-ordinates of whose vertices are A (2, 0), B (4, 5) and C (6, 3).
explanation: Points in the form of line in the given diagram {tex}{/tex} The equation of side AB is, {tex}y-0=\frac{5-0}{4-2}(x-2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{5}{2}(x-2){/tex} The equation of side BC is, {tex}y-3=\frac{5-3}{4-6}(x-6){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y-3=\frac{2}{-2}(x-6){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y-3=-1(x-6){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=-x+9{/tex} The equation of side AC is, {tex}y-0=\frac{3-0}{6-2}(x-2){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{3}{4}(x-2){/tex} Area {tex} = \frac{5}{2}\int_2^4 {(x - 2)dx + \int_4^6 { - (x - 9)dx - \frac{3}{4}\int_2^6 {(x - 2)dx} } } {/tex} {tex}A = \int_2^4 {\frac{5}{2}(x - 2)dx + \int_0^1 { - (x + 9)dx + \int_6^2 {\frac{3}{4}(x - 2)dx} } } {/tex} On integrating we get, {tex}A = \frac{5}{2}\left[ {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} - 2x} \right]_2^4 + \left[ {\frac{{ - {x^2}}}{2} + 9x} \right]_1^0 - \frac{3}{4}\left[ {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} - 2x} \right]_2^6{/tex} On applying limits we get, {tex}A = \frac{5}{2}\left[ {8 - 8 - 2 + 4} \right] + \left[ { - 18 + 54 + 8 - 36} \right] - \frac{3}{4}\left[ {18 - 12 - 2 + 4} \right]{/tex} {tex}A = 5 - 8 - \frac{3}{4}(8){/tex} = 13 - 6 = 7 sq. units. Hence the required area is 7 sq. units.

Points in the form of line in the given diagram

{tex}{/tex}The equation of side AB is,
{tex}y-0=\frac{5-0}{4-2}(x-2){/tex}

explanation: Given region is {(x, y): 9x 2 +4 y 2 {tex}\le{/tex} 36 and 3x + y {tex}\ge{/tex} 6} We draw the curves corresponding to equations 9x 2 + 4y 2 = 36 or {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{4}+\frac{y^{2}}{9}{/tex} = 1 and 3x + y = 6 The curves intersect at (2, 0) and (0, 6) Shaded area is the area enclosed by the two curves and is given as, = {tex}\int_{0}^{2} \sqrt{9\left(1-\frac{x^{2}}{4}\right) d x}{/tex} - {tex}\int_{0}^{2}(6-3 x) d x{/tex} = {tex}3\left[\frac{x}{4} \sqrt{4-x^{2}}\right.{/tex} + {tex}\left.\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}-2 x+\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{2}{/tex} = {tex}3\left[\frac{2}{4} \sqrt{4-4}\right.{/tex} + {tex}\left.\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1} \frac{2}{2}-4+\frac{4}{2}-0\right]{/tex} = {tex}3\left[2 \frac{\pi}{2}-2\right]{/tex} = 3( {tex}\pi{/tex} - 2) square units.

{tex}= \left| {\int\limits_0^\pi {\sin xdx} } \right| + \left| {\int\limits_\pi ^{2\pi } {\sin xdx} } \right|{/tex} 
{tex}= \left| { - \left( {\cos x} \right)_0^\pi } \right| + \left| { - \left( {\cos x} \right)_\pi ^{2\pi }} \right|{/tex} 
{tex}= \left| { - \left( {\cos \pi - \cos 0} \right)} \right| + \left| { - \left( {\cos 2\pi - \cos \pi } \right)} \right|{/tex} 
{tex}= \left| { - \left( { - 1 - 1} \right)} \right| + \left| { - \left( {1 + 1} \right)} \right|{/tex} 
= 2 + 2 = 4 sq. units

question_text: Sketch the graph of y = |x + 3| and evaluate {tex}\int_{ - 6}^0 {\left| {x + 3} \right|} dx{/tex}
explanation: {tex}y = \left| {x + 3} \right|{/tex} {tex}y = \left| {x + 3} \right| = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \begin{gathered} - (x + 3) \hfill \\ x + 3 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} for \hfill \\ for \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} x < - 3 \hfill \\ x \geqslant - 3 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} When x < - 3 y = - x - 3 when x = - 4, y = 4 - 3 = 1 When x = - 5, y = 5 - 3 = 2 When x = - 6, y = 6 - 3 = 3 Required Area = Area of region ABC + Area of region OAD {tex} = \int\limits_6^{ - 3} {\left| {x + 3} \right|dx + \int\limits_{ - 3}^0 {\left| {x + 3} \right|} } dx{/tex} {tex} = \int\limits_6^{ - 3} {\left( { - x - 3} \right)dx + \int\limits_{ - 3}^0 {\left( {x + 3} \right)} } dx{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\frac{{ - {x^2}}}{2} - 3x} \right]_{ - 6}^{ - 3} + \left[ {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} + 3x} \right]_{ - 3}^0{/tex} {tex}=\left[ {\left( { - \frac{9}{2} + 9} \right) - \left( { - 18 + 18} \right)} \right] + \left[ ({0+0)-(\frac{9}{2}} -9)\right]{/tex} {tex} =\ (\frac{9}{2} +0)+(0+ \frac{9}{2}){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 9{/tex} sq. units Hence, the required area is 9 square units.
solution: {tex}y = \left| {x + 3} \right|{/tex} {tex}y = \left| {x + 3} \right| = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \begin{gathered} - (x + 3) \hfill \\ x + 3 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} for \hfill \\ for \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} x < - 3 \hfill \\ x \geqslant - 3 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} When x < - 3 y = - x - 3 when x = - 4, y = 4 - 3 = 1 When x = - 5, y = 5 - 3 = 2 When x = - 6, y = 6 - 3 = 3 Required Area = Area of region ABC + Area of region OAD {tex} = \int\limits_6^{ - 3} {\left| {x + 3} \right|dx + \int\limits_{ - 3}^0 {\left| {x + 3} \right|} } dx{/tex} {tex} = \int\limits_6^{ - 3} {\left( { - x - 3} \right)dx + \int\limits_{ - 3}^0 {\left( {x + 3} \right)} } dx{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\frac{{ - {x^2}}}{2} - 3x} \right]_{ - 6}^{ - 3} + \left[ {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} + 3x} \right]_{ - 3}^0{/tex} {tex}=\left[ {\left( { - \frac{9}{2} + 9} \right) - \left( { - 18 + 18} \right)} \right] + \left[ ({0+0)-(\frac{9}{2}} -9)\right]{/tex} {tex} =\ (\frac{9}{2} +0)+(0+ \frac{9}{2}){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 9{/tex} sq. units Hence, the required area is 9 square units.
solution_html:
{tex}y = \left| {x + 3} \right|{/tex} 
{tex}y = \left| {x + 3} \right| = \left\{ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} \begin{gathered} - (x + 3) \hfill \\ x + 3 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} for \hfill \\ for \hfill \\ \end{gathered} &\begin{gathered} x < - 3 \hfill \\ x \geqslant - 3 \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \end{array}} \right.{/tex} 
When x < - 3
y = - x - 3
when x = - 4,
y = 4 - 3 = 1
When x = - 5,
y = 5 - 3 = 2
When x = - 6,
y = 6 - 3 = 3
Required Area = Area of region ABC + Area of region OAD
{tex} = \int\limits_6^{ - 3} {\left| {x + 3} \right|dx + \int\limits_{ - 3}^0 {\left| {x + 3} \right|} } dx{/tex} 
{tex} = \int\limits_6^{ - 3} {\left( { - x - 3} \right)dx + \int\limits_{ - 3}^0 {\left( {x + 3} \right)} } dx{/tex} 
{tex} = \left[ {\frac{{ - {x^2}}}{2} - 3x} \right]_{ - 6}^{ - 3} + \left[ {\frac{{{x^2}}}{2} + 3x} \right]_{ - 3}^0{/tex} 
{tex}=\left[ {\left( { - \frac{9}{2} + 9} \right) - \left( { - 18 + 18} \right)} \right] + \left[ ({0+0)-(\frac{9}{2}} -9)\right]{/tex} 
{tex} =\ (\frac{9}{2} +0)+(0+ \frac{9}{2}){/tex} 
{tex} \Rightarrow 9{/tex} sq. units
Hence, the required area is 9 square units.

question_html: If the area bounded by the parabola {tex}y^{2}=16 a x{/tex} and the line {tex}y=4 m x{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{12}{/tex} sq. units, then using integration, find the value of m.
question_text: If the area bounded by the parabola {tex}y^{2}=16 a x{/tex} and the line {tex}y=4 m x{/tex} is {tex}\frac{\mathrm{a}^{2}}{12}{/tex} sq. units, then using integration, find the value of m.
explanation: The given equations are : y 2 = 16ax ...(1) y = 4mx ....(2) Equation (1) represent a parabola having centre at the origin and vertex along positive x−axis. Equation (2) represents a straight line passing through the origin and making an angle of 45 with x−axis. POINTS OF INTERSECTION : Put y = 4mx in (1), we get 16m 2 x 2 − 16ax = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16x [m 2 x − a] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0; x = {tex}\frac{a}{m^2}{/tex} When x = 0; y = 0 When x = {tex}\frac{a}{m^{2}}{/tex} , then y = {tex}\frac{4 a}{m}{/tex} Required area =Area under parabola - Area under line = {tex}4 \sqrt{a} \int_{0}^{a / m^{2}} \sqrt{x} d x-4 m \int_{0}^{a / m^{2}} x d x{/tex} {tex}=4 \sqrt{\mathrm{a}} \times \frac{2}{3}\left[\mathrm{x}^{3 \over 2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{a} \over \mathrm{m}^{2}}-\frac{4 \mathrm{m}}{2}\left[\mathrm{x}^{2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{a} \over \mathrm{m}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}-\frac{2 a^{2}}{m^{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}-\frac{2 a^{2}}{m^{3}}=\frac{2}{3}\frac{a^2}{m^3}{/tex} Now, area = {tex}\frac{a^{2}}{12}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{2}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}=\frac{a^{2}}{12}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}^{3}=8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} m = 2
solution: The given equations are : y 2 = 16ax ...(1) y = 4mx ....(2) Equation (1) represent a parabola having centre at the origin and vertex along positive x−axis. Equation (2) represents a straight line passing through the origin and making an angle of 45 with x−axis. POINTS OF INTERSECTION : Put y = 4mx in (1), we get 16m 2 x 2 − 16ax = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16x [m 2 x − a] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0; x = {tex}\frac{a}{m^2}{/tex} When x = 0; y = 0 When x = {tex}\frac{a}{m^{2}}{/tex} , then y = {tex}\frac{4 a}{m}{/tex} Required area =Area under parabola - Area under line = {tex}4 \sqrt{a} \int_{0}^{a / m^{2}} \sqrt{x} d x-4 m \int_{0}^{a / m^{2}} x d x{/tex} {tex}=4 \sqrt{\mathrm{a}} \times \frac{2}{3}\left[\mathrm{x}^{3 \over 2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{a} \over \mathrm{m}^{2}}-\frac{4 \mathrm{m}}{2}\left[\mathrm{x}^{2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{a} \over \mathrm{m}^{2}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}-\frac{2 a^{2}}{m^{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{8}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}-\frac{2 a^{2}}{m^{3}}=\frac{2}{3}\frac{a^2}{m^3}{/tex} Now, area = {tex}\frac{a^{2}}{12}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{2}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}=\frac{a^{2}}{12}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}^{3}=8{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} m = 2
solution_html:
The given equations are :
y2 = 16ax   ...(1)
y = 4mx ....(2)
Equation (1) represent a parabola having centre at the origin and vertex along positive x−axis.
Equation (2) represents a straight line passing through the origin and making an angle of 45 with x−axis.
POINTS OF INTERSECTION :
Put y = 4mx in (1), we get
16m2x2 − 16ax = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 16x [m2x − a] = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 0; x = {tex}\frac{a}{m^2}{/tex}
When x = 0; y = 0
When x = {tex}\frac{a}{m^{2}}{/tex}, then y = {tex}\frac{4 a}{m}{/tex}

Required area =Area under parabola - Area under line

= {tex}4 \sqrt{a} \int_{0}^{a / m^{2}} \sqrt{x} d x-4 m \int_{0}^{a / m^{2}} x d x{/tex}
{tex}=4 \sqrt{\mathrm{a}} \times \frac{2}{3}\left[\mathrm{x}^{3 \over 2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{a} \over \mathrm{m}^{2}}-\frac{4 \mathrm{m}}{2}\left[\mathrm{x}^{2}\right]_{0}^{\mathrm{a} \over \mathrm{m}^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{8}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}-\frac{2 a^{2}}{m^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{8}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}-\frac{2 a^{2}}{m^{3}}=\frac{2}{3}\frac{a^2}{m^3}{/tex}
Now, area = {tex}\frac{a^{2}}{12}{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{2}{3} \frac{a^{2}}{m^{3}}=\frac{a^{2}}{12}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{m}^{3}=8{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} m = 2

explanation: {tex}{x^2} + {y^2} = 4{/tex} ...(1) x + y = 2 ...(2) From (2), y = 2 - x Put this value of y in (1), we get, x 2 + (2 - x) 2 = 4 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 2x 2 - 4x = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} 2x(x - 2) = 0 {tex} \Rightarrow {/tex} x = 0, 2 When x = 0, y = 2 - 0 = 2 When x = 2, y = 2 - 2 = 0 {tex}\therefore {/tex} points of intersection are (0, 2) and (2, 0) Required area = area of quadrant in first quadrant - area of triangle. {tex} = \int_0^2 {\sqrt {4 - {x^2}} dx - \int_0^2 {(2 - x)dx} } {/tex} {tex} = \int_0^2 {\sqrt {{{(2)}^2} - {x^2}} dx - \int_0^2 {(2 - x)dx} } {/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\frac{x}{2}\sqrt {{{\left( 2 \right)}^2} - {x^2}} + \frac{{{{\left( 2 \right)}^2}}}{2}{{\sin }^{ - 1}}\frac{x}{2}} \right]_0^2 - \left[ {2x - \frac{{{x^2}}}{2}} \right]_0^2{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\frac{2}{2}\sqrt {4 - 4} + \frac{4}{2}{{\sin }^{ - 1}}1} \right] - \left[ {0 + \frac{4}{2}{{\sin }^{ - 1}}0} \right] - \left[ {\left({4 - \frac{4}{2}} \right) - (0 - 0)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left( {0 + \frac{4}{2} \times \frac{\pi }{2}} \right) - (0 + 2 \times 0) - 2 + 0 = \pi - 2{/tex}

question_html: Find the area enclosed by the parabola 4y = 3x2 and the line 2y = 3x + 12
question_text: Find the area enclosed by the parabola 4y = 3x 2 and the line 2y = 3x + 12
explanation: Equation of the parabola is 4y = 3x 2 …(i) {tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} = \frac{4}{3}y{/tex} Equation of the line is 2y = 3x + 12 …(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac{{3x + 12}}{2} = \frac{{3x}}{2} + 6{/tex} In the graph, points of intersection are B (4, 12) and C (-2, 3). Now, Area ABCD {tex}= \left| {\int\limits_{ - 2}^4 {\left( {\frac{3}{2}x + 6} \right)dx} } \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\frac{3}{4}{x^2} + 6x} \right]_{ - 2}^4{/tex} = (12 + 24) - (3 - 12) = 45 sq units Again, Area CDO + Area OAB {tex}= \left| {\int\limits_{ - 2}^4 {\left( {\frac{3}{4}{x^2}} \right)dx} } \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\frac{3}{4}.\frac{{{x^3}}}{3}} \right]_{ - 2}^4{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{4}\left[ {64 - \left( { - 8} \right)} \right] = 18{/tex} sq. units {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area = Area ABCD - (Area CDO + Area OAB) = 45 - 18 = 27 sq. units
solution: Equation of the parabola is 4y = 3x 2 …(i) {tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} = \frac{4}{3}y{/tex} Equation of the line is 2y = 3x + 12 …(ii) {tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac{{3x + 12}}{2} = \frac{{3x}}{2} + 6{/tex} In the graph, points of intersection are B (4, 12) and C (-2, 3). Now, Area ABCD {tex}= \left| {\int\limits_{ - 2}^4 {\left( {\frac{3}{2}x + 6} \right)dx} } \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\frac{3}{4}{x^2} + 6x} \right]_{ - 2}^4{/tex} = (12 + 24) - (3 - 12) = 45 sq units Again, Area CDO + Area OAB {tex}= \left| {\int\limits_{ - 2}^4 {\left( {\frac{3}{4}{x^2}} \right)dx} } \right|{/tex} {tex}= \left[ {\frac{3}{4}.\frac{{{x^3}}}{3}} \right]_{ - 2}^4{/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{4}\left[ {64 - \left( { - 8} \right)} \right] = 18{/tex} sq. units {tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area = Area ABCD - (Area CDO + Area OAB) = 45 - 18 = 27 sq. units
solution_html:
Equation of the parabola is
4y = 3x2 …(i)

{tex} \Rightarrow {x^2} = \frac{4}{3}y{/tex}

Equation of the line is 2y = 3x + 12 …(ii)
{tex} \Rightarrow y = \frac{{3x + 12}}{2} = \frac{{3x}}{2} + 6{/tex} 
In the graph, points of intersection are B (4, 12) and C (-2, 3).
Now, Area ABCD {tex}= \left| {\int\limits_{ - 2}^4 {\left( {\frac{3}{2}x + 6} \right)dx} } \right|{/tex} 
{tex}= \left[ {\frac{3}{4}{x^2} + 6x} \right]_{ - 2}^4{/tex} 
= (12 + 24) - (3 - 12)
= 45 sq units
Again, Area CDO + Area OAB {tex}= \left| {\int\limits_{ - 2}^4 {\left( {\frac{3}{4}{x^2}} \right)dx} } \right|{/tex} 
{tex}= \left[ {\frac{3}{4}.\frac{{{x^3}}}{3}} \right]_{ - 2}^4{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{4}\left[ {64 - \left( { - 8} \right)} \right] = 18{/tex} sq. units
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Required area = Area ABCD - (Area CDO + Area OAB)
= 45 - 18 = 27 sq. units

According to the question ,
Given parabola is y2 = x......(i)
vertex of parabola is  ( 0, 0)

axis of parabola lies along X-axis.
Given equation of line is x + y = 2.......(ii)
For, x + y = 2

x 2 0
y 0 2

So, line passes through the points (2, 0) and (0, 2).
Now, let us sketch the graph of given curve and line as shown below:

On putting x = 2 - y from Eq. (ii) in Eq. (i), we get
{tex} y^2 = 2 - y {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow y^2 + y - 2 = 0 {/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow y^2 + 2y - y - 2 = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow y(y + 2) -1(y + 2) = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow ( y - 1 ) ( y + 2) = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  y = 1 or -2

When y = -2, then x = 2 - y = 2 - (- 2) = 4

Now, required area = {tex}\int _ { - 2 } ^ { 1 } \left[ x _ { ( line ) } - x _ { \text { (parabola) } } \right] d y{/tex}

{tex}= \left( 2 - \frac { 1 } { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 3 } \right) - \left( - 4 - 2 + \frac { 8 } { 3 } \right){/tex}
{tex}= 2 - \frac { 5 } { 6 } + 6 - \frac { 8 } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}= 8 - \frac { 5 } { 6 } - \frac { 8 } { 3 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 48 - 5 - 16 } { 6 } {/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 48 - 21 } { 6 }{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 27 } { 6 } {/tex}
